title: Quenched Dipole Pairs in Viscous Fluid Membranes across the Saffman Crossover: Integrable Hamiltonian Dynamics
authors: Satyagni Bhattacharya, Debdatta Dey, Samyak Jain, Yassir Khan, Tirthankar Mazumder, Aryaman Mihir Seth, Nikhil Mogalapalli, Divyansh Tiwari, Pravallika Vemparala, Rickmoy Samanta
abstract: We investigate an analytic theory of force-dipole hydrodynamics in a viscous membrane coupled to an infinite surrounding fluid, focusing on quenched (orientation-fixed) dipoles. While the single-dipole flow exhibits the known Saffman crossover from a near-field \(v\sim r^{-1}\) to a screened far-field \(v\sim r^{-2}\), we show that this crossover induces a qualitatively new reorganization of dipole--dipole interactions. For two identical quenched dipoles, the near-field dynamics is exactly solvable and effectively one-dimensional, with a fixed line of centers and linear evolution of the squared separation. In the far field, the system remains integrable but becomes intrinsically two-dimensional, with coupled radial and angular dynamics and an exact first integral. For pullers, the angular dynamics drives alignment toward an attracting manifold, leading to universal late-time collapse \(R\sim (t_c-t)^{1/3}\), in contrast to the near-field scaling \(R\sim (t_c-t)^{1/2}\). The Saffman crossover thus reorganizes the Hamiltonian phase-space structure of dipolar interactions and produces a transition from effectively one-dimensional to fully coupled dynamics, providing a minimal framework for aggregation in viscous fluid membranes.
summary: This paper explores how hydrodynamic screening in fluid membranes changes the way active particles interact. It shows that the Saffman crossover fundamentally changes the nature of the interaction from a simple 1D-like radial motion to a complex 2D coupled motion. For 'puller' type particles, this leads to a predictable, universal way that particles aggregate over time. This work provides a minimal mathematical framework for understanding how momentum leakage into a surrounding fluid can reorganize the dynamics of embedded inclusions.
main_problem: The paper investigates how the Saffman crossover (the transition from near-field to far-field hydrodynamic flow) reorganizes the interaction and phase-space structure of quenched force-dipole pairs in viscous membranes.
main_result: The Saffman crossover induces a transition from effectively 1D dynamics (where angular mismatch is conserved) to 2D dynamics (with coupled radial and angular evolution), leading to a universal cubic collapse law for pullers in the far-field.
method: The authors use an analytical approach involving 2D Fourier transforms, derivation of the membrane Green's function, and asymptotic analysis of the resulting hydrodynamic equations within a Hamiltonian framework.
model_or_system: A 2D incompressible, viscous membrane coupled to an infinite 3D surrounding fluid, containing two identical, orientation-fixed (quenched) force-dipoles (either pullers or pushers).
key_observables: Velocity fields, vorticity fields, relative separation (R), angular evolution (psi), and the scaling of the collapse time (tc).
important_parameters: Saffman length (lambda), dipole strength (sigma), membrane viscosity (eta_s), and the distinction between pullers (sigma > 0) and pushers (sigma < 0).
main_assumptions: Flat membrane geometry, infinite subphase, low Reynolds number (Stokes flow), incompressible 2D flow, and fixed dipole orientations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 visualizes the velocity fields of a single puller dipole, comparing the purely radial near-field flow to the rotational far-field flow. Figure 2 provides a schematic of the two-dipole system and their relative coordinates.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical problem of dipolar interactions, derives the hydrodynamic Green's function for the membrane, analyzes the near-field and far-field asymptotic regimes, constructs a Hamiltonian description of the two-body problem, and concludes with the derivation of universal scaling laws for particle aggregation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
